category_title: Learners with ADD who overestimate their emotional competence and struggle with social-emotional processing
minimal_context: The student is working in a group in the classroom to act out different emotional scenarios from the curriculum.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Alright everyone, for this activity, you need to work in your groups to act out the different emotional scenarios from the lesson.

STUDENT: I'm going to be the director and the main character. I'll tell everyone else where to stand and what to say because I know exactly how to do this.

TUTOR: Try to work together as a team.

STUDENT: I am! Look at John, he's sighing and crossing his arms, which means he's just waiting for me to give him his lines.

TUTOR: What do you think John is really feeling? Keep trying.

STUDENT: He's fine! (Grabs John's arm and pulls him forward) Stand here and do the sad face like I told you! I don't get why you're getting mad and yelling at me to stop, I'm just making you act!

options.A: Create a seating arrangement so the student is always part of a group, and rotate the student among different groups.
options.B: Encourage students to assume leadership roles and enable groups to assign these themselves, offering a range of roles.
options.C: Place special emphasis on the developmental integration of affect, the vocabulary of emotion, and cognitive understanding.
options.D: Conduct a functional assessment of the student's behavior during the group acting activities.
